These become excellent material for Task 6 (AI Reflection).

One thing I already noticed

The requirements leave several behaviors unspecified, for example:

Should matching be case-sensitive or case-insensitive?

What happens if the input is empty?

Are duplicate suggestions allowed?

Can the user press Enter to select a suggestion, or only to submit?

What happens if no suggestion matches?

Can users submit arbitrary text, or must it exactly match a suggestion? (FR-01 says users can type any response, while FR-04 mentions invalid input-this is an ambiguity worth documenting.)

Should leading/trailing spaces be trimmed before matching?

What happens if the backend configuration switches between prefix match and match-anywhere while the page is open?

A good QA engineer calls out these ambiguities instead of making assumptions.

I think we can make this stand out

Instead of submitting an average assignment, we can make it look like something submitted by an experienced SDET.

We'll include:

Clean Markdown documentation

Proper Playwright architecture

Professional API tests

Risk-based testing rationale

Well-structured test cases

Thoughtful requirement analysis

Realistic AI reflection

It'll be polished enough to serve as a portfolio piece afterward.

Yes-and this is exactly the kind of thing interviewers like to see. A good SDET doesn't just test what's written; they identify what's not written. The assignment is intentionally leaving some behavior undefined to see whether you notice it. _Practical Assignment_SDET.pdf

Here's a requirement analysis you could use as the basis for 1-requirement-analysis.md.

Requirement Analysis

Overview

The application is an autocomplete form where a user can either type a value or select one from a dynamically filtered suggestion list. Once the user submits the form, the selected/entered value is persisted through a REST API and the UI displays either a success or error message.

The specification defines the core functional behavior but leaves several implementation details unspecified. These ambiguities should be clarified with the product owner before testing or development begins.

Functional Requirement Analysis

FR-01 - Text Input

Expected Behaviour

The user should be able to:

Type directly into the input field.

Select a suggestion by clicking/tapping a suggestion.

The input field should update as the user types or after selecting a suggestion.

Ambiguity

This requirement states:

Users can type any response OR click/tap a suggestion.

However FR-04 states:

On invalid input, an error message is displayed.

This creates a contradiction.

It is unclear what constitutes a valid input.

Possible interpretations are:

Interpretation
Result
Any text is accepted
Every submission succeeds
Input must exactly equal one of the suggestions
Any other text is invalid
Input only needs to match a filtered suggestion
Partial matches may be accepted
Backend validates according to additional business rules
UI alone cannot determine validity

This is the largest ambiguity in the specification and directly impacts both UI and API test cases.

FR-02 - Suggestion Filtering (Prefix Match)

Expected Behaviour

As the user types:

Suggestions beginning with the typed characters remain visible.

Suggestions that do not start with the typed characters disappear.

Example

Input:

agi

Visible

agile methodology
agile methodology process
agile methodology process testing

Input

method

Visible

None

because no suggestion starts with "method".

Missing Details

The specification does not define:

whether matching is case-sensitive

whether spaces are ignored

whether leading/trailing spaces are trimmed

whether special characters are allowed

whether filtering occurs after every keystroke or after a delay

whether suggestions should reappear when characters are deleted

FR-03 - Suggestion Filtering (Match Anywhere)

Expected Behaviour

When backend configuration enables Match Anywhere:

A suggestion remains visible if the typed value occurs anywhere inside the suggestion.

Example

Typing

method

would display

agile methodology
agile methodology process
agile methodology process testing

Missing Details

The specification never explains

how the configuration is enabled

whether the configuration changes without refreshing the page

whether frontend knows the current mode

whether API exposes the active configuration

These assumptions affect automation.

FR-04 - Form Submission

Expected Behaviour

Selecting Next

↓

REST API request

↓

If response == HTTP 200

↓

Display success message

Otherwise

↓

Display error message

Ambiguities

The specification does not define

Which HTTP status code represents invalid input

Whether non-200 responses display the same error

Whether network failures are handled differently

Whether the button becomes disabled during submission

Whether duplicate submissions are prevented

Whether the request is retried

Whether loading indicators exist

FR-05 - Backend Data Contract

The API response must contain

account_id

account_email

start_date

end_date

locale

text

suggestion_list

completed

Missing Details

The specification defines the required fields but omits several validation rules.

Examples:

account_id

numeric or string?

UUID?

nullable?

start_date / end_date

States

user's local time

But does not specify

ISO-8601?

timezone offset?

milliseconds?

UTC allowed?

locale

States

IETF BCP 47

but does not specify

language only?

language-region mandatory?

lowercase vs uppercase region?

Example shows

en-IN

which implies region should exist.

suggestion_list

Requirement says

matching suggestions

It never defines

current visible suggestions?

all suggestions?

only selected suggestion?

comma-space separator or comma only?

completed

States Boolean.

Does not define

true after successful upload only?

false before upload?

omitted on failure?

Overall Business Logic

Based on the requirements, the expected flow appears to be:

User opens page
        │
        ▼
Input field available
        │
        ▼
User types
        │
        ▼
Suggestions filtered
        │
        ▼
User selects suggestion
OR continues typing
        │
        ▼
Clicks Next
        │
        ▼
REST API request
        │
        ├── Valid
        │      │
        │      ▼
        │ HTTP 200
        │      │
        │      ▼
        │ Success Message
        │
        └── Invalid
               │
               ▼
         Error Message

Identified Requirement Gaps / Questions

#
Observation
Impact
1
Definition of a valid input is missing
Critical
2
Case sensitivity of matching is undefined
High
3
Whitespace handling is unspecified
Medium
4
Empty input behavior is undefined
High
5
Duplicate suggestions are not discussed
Medium
6
No behavior defined when zero suggestions remain
Medium
7
No keyboard navigation requirements for suggestions
Medium
8
HTTP error handling beyond 200 is missing
High
9
API timestamp format is not fully specified
Medium
10
Meaning of suggestion_list ("matching") is ambiguous
High
11
Backend configuration mechanism for filtering mode is unspecified
Medium
12
Maximum input length and allowed characters are not defined
Low

Most Significant Ambiguity

The most important ambiguity is the definition of valid input.

FR-01 explicitly allows users to type any response, suggesting free-form input is acceptable. However, FR-04 introduces an invalid input error without defining the validation criteria. Because the autocomplete suggestions are the only known data source, a reasonable assumption is that the submitted text must correspond to one of the available suggestions (likely an exact match), but the specification never confirms this. This ambiguity directly affects UI validation, API behavior, expected HTTP responses, and the design of positive and negative test cases.

I actually like this assignment because it's written like a real-world specification: it defines the happy path but leaves enough gaps for a QA engineer to ask questions instead of making assumptions. Identifying these ambiguities-and clearly documenting them rather than guessing-is exactly the kind of analytical thinking interviewers look for in an SDET.

I think this document should not become a restatement of the functional requirements. Many candidates will simply copy FR-01 to FR-05 into Markdown.

A good Requirement Analysis document should answer:

"What did I understand from the requirements before I started testing?"

Think of it as a QA engineer reading the spec and documenting their understanding, assumptions, and questions.

I would structure 1-requirement-analysis.md like this

1. Objective
2. Scope
3. Functional Requirements Summary
4. Business Flow
5. Key Functional Logic
6. Assumptions
7. Requirement Ambiguities
8. Risks & Clarifications Required

Example

✓ Autocomplete input

✓ Form submission
✓ Backend data contract

Out of Scope

✗ Admin configuration

The assignment explicitly states login and admin configuration are outside the scope. _Practical Assignment_SDET.pdf

or continues typing

Interviewers LOVE flow diagrams.

5. Key Functional Logic

This is where you explain the feature in your own words.

For example

Input

accepts typing

Filtering

Configurable Mode

typed text

visible suggestions

Submission

Success

6. Assumptions

These are assumptions you make because the requirements don't explicitly say so.

For example:

Assumption
Reason
Suggestions update while typing
Typical autocomplete behavior
Filtering occurs after every keystroke
Not specified but expected
Backend validates submitted input
Error message implies validation
start_date is recorded when page loads
Requirement says "reached the form"
end_date is recorded when Next is clicked
Directly stated

7. Requirement Ambiguities ⭐

This is probably the most important section.

Instead of writing a giant paragraph, make a table.

Requirement
Observation
Impact
FR-01 & FR-04
"Any response" conflicts with "Invalid input"
Critical
FR-02
Case sensitivity not defined

FR-02

This looks very professional.

8. Risks / Clarifications Required

These are the questions you'd ask a Business Analyst or Product Owner.

Examples:

What exactly is considered a valid input?

Must submitted text exactly match a suggestion?

Are partial matches accepted?

Is filtering case-sensitive?

Is matching trimmed before comparison?

What happens when no suggestions exist?

Which HTTP status code indicates invalid input?

Should pressing Enter submit or select a suggestion?

Should duplicate submissions be prevented?

What should suggestion_list contain?

all suggestions

visible suggestions

selected suggestion

What I would NOT include

I wouldn't put:

Detailed test cases

Test scenarios

Defects from Question 2

Playwright code

API validations

Those belong in later sections.

The finished document should feel like this

1. Objective

2. Scope

3. Functional Requirement Summary

4. Business Flow

5. Key Functional Logic

6. Assumptions

7. Requirement Ambiguities

8. Clarifications Required

That would be around 3-4 pages, which is about right for a requirement analysis. It's concise, analytical, and sets a solid foundation for the remaining tasks rather than duplicating the specification.
give be the md?

Absolutely! Below is a polished docs/1-requirement-analysis.md that looks professional while still reflecting your own analysis. It focuses on understanding the requirements, business logic, assumptions, and ambiguities, rather than repeating the specification.

# Requirement Analysis

## Objective

This document summarizes the functional requirements, business logic, assumptions, and identified ambiguities for the Autocomplete Form application. The goal is to establish a clear understanding of the expected system behavior before designing test scenarios, test cases, and automation scripts.

---

# Scope

## In Scope

- Autocomplete text input
- Suggestion filtering
- Suggestion selection
- Form submission
- Backend response data contract

## Out of Scope

- Login functionality
- Admin configuration for autocomplete behavior

---

# Functional Requirements Summary

| Requirement | Summary |
|-------------|---------|
| **FR-01** | Users can either type into the input field or select a suggestion from the list. |
| **FR-02** | Suggestions are filtered using **Prefix Match** by default. |
| **FR-03** | Backend configuration can switch filtering to **Match Anywhere** mode. |
| **FR-04** | Clicking **Next** submits the response through a REST API and displays either a success or error message. |
| **FR-05** | The backend stores user information, timestamps, locale, entered text, matching suggestions, and upload status. |

---

# Business Flow

```text
User opens Autocomplete Form
            │
            ▼
     User enters text
            │
            ▼
 Suggestions are filtered
(Prefix Match / Match Anywhere)
            │
            ▼
User selects a suggestion
or continues typing
            │
            ▼
       Click Next
            │
            ▼
     REST API Request
            │
      ┌─────┴─────┐
      ▼           ▼
 HTTP 200      Invalid Input
      │           │
      ▼           ▼
 Success      Error Message
```

---

# Functional Logic

## 1. User Input

The application allows users to either:

- Type directly into the input field.
- Select a suggestion from the autocomplete list.

---

## 2. Suggestion Filtering

### Default Mode (Prefix Match)

Suggestions remain visible only when they **start with** the typed characters.

Example:

Input:

```
agi
```

Visible Suggestions:

```
agile methodology
agile methodology process
agile methodology process testing
```

---

### Configurable Mode (Match Anywhere)

When enabled through backend configuration, suggestions remain visible if they **contain** the typed text anywhere within the suggestion.

Example:

Input:

```
method
```

Visible Suggestions:

```
agile methodology
agile methodology process
agile methodology process testing
```

---

## 3. Form Submission

Clicking the **Next** button sends a REST API request to persist the response.

Expected behavior:

- Successful submission returns **HTTP 200**.
- Success message is displayed.
- Invalid submission displays an error message.

---

# Assumptions

The specification does not explicitly define several implementation details. The following assumptions have been identified:

- Suggestions are filtered dynamically as the user types.
- Filtering occurs after every keystroke.
- Selecting a suggestion populates the input field.
- `start_date` is recorded when the user reaches the form.
- `end_date` is recorded when the user clicks **Next**.
- Backend performs validation before accepting the submitted response.

---

# Requirement Ambiguities

The specification contains several areas that require clarification before implementation or testing.

| Requirement | Observation | Impact |
|-------------|-------------|--------|
| **FR-01 & FR-04** | FR-01 states users can type **any response**, while FR-04 mentions **invalid input** without defining what makes an input invalid. | **Critical** |
| **FR-02** | Case sensitivity of filtering is not specified. | High |
| **FR-02** | Behavior for empty input is not defined (show all suggestions or none). | High |
| **FR-02** | Handling of leading/trailing spaces is unspecified. | Medium |
| **FR-02** | Maximum input length and allowed special characters are not specified. | Medium |
| **FR-03** | Backend configuration mechanism for switching filtering modes is not described. | Medium |
| **FR-04** | Expected HTTP status codes for failed submissions are not specified. | High |
| **FR-04** | Loading indicators, retry behavior, and duplicate submission handling are not mentioned. | Medium |
| **FR-05** | Timestamp format is not fully defined despite mentioning the user's local time. | Medium |
| **FR-05** | The exact meaning of `suggestion_list` ("matching suggestions") is ambiguous. | High |

---

# Clarifications Required

The following questions should be clarified with the Product Owner or Business Analyst before implementation or testing.

1. What exactly qualifies as a **valid input**?
   - Can users submit any text?
   - Or must the text exactly match one of the suggestions?

2. Is suggestion matching **case-sensitive**?

3. Should leading and trailing spaces be ignored before matching?

4. What happens when the input field is empty?
   - Show all suggestions?
   - Show none?

5. What should happen when no suggestions match the entered text?

6. Does pressing **Enter** submit the form or select the highlighted suggestion?

7. What HTTP status code should be returned for invalid input?

8. Should duplicate form submissions be prevented?

9. What should `suggestion_list` contain?
   - All available suggestions?
   - Only currently visible (matching) suggestions?
   - Only the selected suggestion?

10. What is the exact timestamp format expected for `start_date` and `end_date`?

11. Is the locale expected to always include both language and region (e.g., `en-IN`)?

---

# Key Observation

The most significant ambiguity in the specification is the definition of **valid input**.

FR-01 states that users can **type any response**, suggesting that free-text input is allowed. However, FR-04 specifies that an **error message is displayed for invalid input**, without defining the validation criteria.

Based on the available requirements, it is reasonable to assume that the backend validates the submitted value and may require it to correspond to one of the available suggestions. However, the specification does not explicitly confirm whether:

- An exact match is required,
- A partial match is sufficient, or
- Any free-text value is considered valid.

This ambiguity directly impacts UI validation, API validation, expected response codes, and the design of positive and negative test scenarios.

I think this version is strong because it reads like something an SDET would actually produce during the analysis phase. It doesn't simply restate the requirements—it identifies assumptions, raises meaningful questions, and explains where the specification could lead to different implementations. That's exactly the kind of critical thinking companies are usually looking for in assignments like this.
for excerise 1

For Exercise 1, I think they're evaluating your test design and prioritization skills, not your ability to list every possible test. The keyword is:

"Top 10 test scenarios, ranked from highest to lowest risk." _Practical Assignment_SDET.pdf

So don't think "What are 10 scenarios?".
Think "If this application went to production today, what failures would have the biggest business impact?"

My proposed ranking

Rank
Test Scenario
Risk
Why?
1
Verify valid input can be successfully submitted and persisted
Critical
The application's primary purpose is to collect and save user responses. If submission fails, the feature is unusable.
2
Verify invalid input is rejected with an appropriate error message
Critical
Accepting invalid data or rejecting valid data compromises data integrity and highlights the specification's biggest ambiguity.
3
Verify default Prefix Match filtering
High
Autocomplete is the application's core functionality. Incorrect filtering directly affects usability and selection accuracy.
4
Verify Match Anywhere filtering when enabled
High
This configurable feature changes business behavior. Incorrect implementation would violate configuration requirements.
5
Verify selecting a suggestion populates the input correctly
High
Users are expected to select suggestions. If selection fails, submission may also fail.
6
Verify API payload matches the FR-05 data contract
High
Incorrect or incomplete persisted data impacts downstream systems and reporting.
7
Verify keyboard accessibility (Tab, Enter, Escape)
Medium
Keyboard interaction is an explicit automation requirement and important for accessibility.
8
Verify suggestion list updates dynamically while typing
Medium
Ensures filtering reacts correctly to user input and provides a smooth user experience.
9
Verify behavior when no suggestions match
Medium
Common user scenario that should be handled gracefully without errors.
10
Verify success and error messages display correctly
Low
Feedback is important for usability, but incorrect messaging has less business impact than failures in filtering or data persistence.

Why this ordering?

I intentionally ranked them based on business risk, not execution order.

Critical

These directly affect whether the application fulfills its purpose.

Can users submit data?

Is invalid data prevented?

Without these, the application fails its primary objective.

High

These cover the core autocomplete functionality.

If filtering or suggestion selection doesn't work, users cannot reliably enter valid data.

The API contract is also High because downstream systems depend on accurate persisted data.

Medium

These affect usability and completeness but don't necessarily prevent the main workflow.

Examples:

Keyboard navigation

Dynamic filtering updates

No-match scenarios

Low

UI feedback (success/error messages) is important but secondary.

If the backend stores data correctly but the message is incorrect, the core functionality still works.

One thing I'd tweak from the assignment

Notice that the assignment asks for test scenarios, not test cases.

A scenario should stay high level.

For example:

✅ Good Scenario

Verify autocomplete suggestions are filtered using prefix matching.

❌ Too detailed

Type "agi" into the input field, verify only three suggestions remain visible, click Next...

Those detailed steps belong in Exercise 3 (Test Cases).

I think this ranking is defensible in an interview because each scenario is prioritized by business impact, likelihood of failure, and effect on the end-to-end user journey, rather than simply following the order of the functional requirements. That demonstrates risk-based testing, which is exactly what SDET interviewers often look for.
the MD  please

Sure! Here's a polished docs/2-test-scenarios.md in Markdown format.

# Test Scenarios (Risk-Based Prioritization)

## Overview

The following test scenarios have been prioritized using a **risk-based testing** approach. The ranking considers the application's primary business objective, likelihood of failure, impact on users, and impact on data integrity.

---

| Rank | Test Scenario | Risk Level | Rationale |
|:---:|---------------|:----------:|-----------|
| **1** | Verify a valid input can be successfully submitted and persisted via the REST API. | **Critical** | The application's primary purpose is to collect and persist user responses. If submission fails, the core functionality is unusable. |
| **2** | Verify invalid input is rejected and an appropriate error message is displayed. | **Critical** | Invalid data should not be persisted. Incorrect validation may lead to data integrity issues. The specification also contains ambiguity regarding what constitutes a valid input. |
| **3** | Verify autocomplete suggestions are filtered correctly using **Prefix Match** (default mode). | **High** | Prefix filtering is the primary autocomplete behavior. Incorrect filtering directly impacts usability and suggestion accuracy. |
| **4** | Verify autocomplete suggestions are filtered correctly using **Match Anywhere** mode when enabled. | **High** | This configurable behavior changes the application's filtering logic. Failure would violate business requirements when the configuration is enabled. |
| **5** | Verify selecting a suggestion correctly populates the input field. | **High** | Suggestion selection is one of the primary interaction methods. Incorrect population may lead to incorrect or failed submissions. |
| **6** | Verify the submitted API response conforms to the FR-05 backend data contract. | **High** | Persisted data must contain the correct fields, formats, and data types for downstream systems to function correctly. |
| **7** | Verify keyboard interactions (Tab, Enter, Escape) function correctly throughout the form. | **Medium** | Keyboard accessibility improves usability and is explicitly required for automation testing, but does not directly affect business logic. |
| **8** | Verify suggestions dynamically update as the user types or deletes characters. | **Medium** | Dynamic filtering provides the expected autocomplete experience. Failure impacts usability but not data persistence. |
| **9** | Verify application behavior when no suggestions match the entered text. | **Medium** | The application should gracefully handle cases where no suggestions are available without unexpected behavior or crashes. |
| **10** | Verify appropriate success and error messages are displayed after form submission. | **Low** | User feedback is important for usability, but incorrect messaging has lower business impact than failures affecting filtering or data persistence. |

---

# Risk Assessment Summary

## Critical

These scenarios validate the application's primary business objective—accepting valid responses while preventing invalid data from being stored.

- Successful form submission
- Invalid input handling

---

## High

These scenarios verify the core autocomplete functionality and backend data integrity.

- Prefix filtering
- Match Anywhere filtering
- Suggestion selection
- API data contract validation

---

## Medium

These scenarios focus on usability, accessibility, and edge-case behavior.

- Keyboard navigation
- Dynamic filtering updates
- No matching suggestions

---

## Low

These scenarios verify user feedback after submission.

- Success message
- Error message

Although important for user experience, failures in these areas do not prevent the core functionality of the application.

💡 One suggestion

If this were a real interview submission, I'd make Scenario #2 a little more explicit because it's the biggest ambiguity in the specification. Instead of:

Verify invalid input is rejected...

I'd write:

Verify only valid inputs are accepted and invalid inputs are rejected according to the application's validation rules.

This subtly tells the reviewer that you noticed the specification never actually defines what a valid input is—a nice piece of analytical thinking without sounding argumentative.

i have the following
TC-01 — Valid selection persists a record matching the FR-05 contract exactly
Title: Submitting a clicked suggestion creates a record with all FR-05 fields, correct types
Preconditions: User logged in, on /autocomplete-form, default prefix-match mode active
Test Steps:
Navigate to /autocomplete-form
Click the suggestion agile methodology
Click Next
GET the persisted response via the API
Expected Results:
UI shows the success message, not the error message
API returns HTTP 200 on POST
GET response contains all 8 FR-05 fields
completed is JSON boolean true (not string)
locale matches en-IN (region-qualified BCP 47)
account_id = 98765, account_email = test123@gmail.com
Test Data: Suggestion clicked: agile methodology
TC-02 — completed is returned as boolean type, never a string
Title: Data type check on completed field
Preconditions: At least one successful submission exists
Test Steps:
Submit a valid suggestion
Inspect the raw JSON response body (not just a truthy check)
Expected Results: typeof response.completed === 'boolean'; value is true
Test Data: N/A (schema/type check)
TC-03 — Invalid free-typed text does not persist a record
Title: Clicking Next with unmatched free text shows error and creates no backend record
Preconditions: User on /autocomplete-form, no prior submissions this session
Test Steps:
Type this is not a real suggestion into the input field
Click Next
Query the API for the latest response (or record count before/after)
Expected Results:
UI shows the error message, not the success message
No new record is created in the backend (count unchanged, or POST returns non-200 e.g. 422)
Test Data: Typed text: this is not a real suggestion
TC-04 — suggestion_list reflects only the filtered/matching suggestions at submit time
Title: suggestion_list excludes suggestions that don't match the submitted text
Preconditions: Prefix-match mode (default) active
Test Steps:
Type agile methodology process t (matches only the third suggestion by prefix)
Click the remaining visible suggestion to select it
Click Next
GET the persisted record
Expected Results: suggestion_list = "agile methodology process testing" only — not all three
Test Data: Typed prefix: agile methodology process t
TC-05 — Locale is captured as the full region-qualified value, not language-only
Title: Persisted locale matches the environment's actual BCP 47 locale (en-IN)
Preconditions: Browser configured as English, user location India
Test Steps:
Submit a valid suggestion
GET the persisted record
Expected Results: locale = en-IN (or the browser's actual navigator.language value for this environment) — not bare en
Test Data: N/A
TC-06 — FR-03 match-anywhere mode surfaces suggestions containing the typed substring anywhere
Title: Match-anywhere config shows suggestions matching mid-string, not just prefix
Preconditions: Backend config matchMode set to anywhere
Test Steps:
Set backend config to match-anywhere mode
Navigate to /autocomplete-form
Type methodology process (not a prefix of any suggestion, but a substring of two)
Expected Results: Suggestions containing the substring anywhere remain visible: agile methodology process and agile methodology process testing; agile methodology is hidden (doesn't contain the full substring)
Test Data: Typed text: methodology process; config: matchMode=anywhere
TC-07 — Full keyboard-only flow: Tab to suggestion, Enter to submit
Title: User can complete the form using only the keyboard
Preconditions: Page freshly loaded, no prior focus
Test Steps:
Press Tab — focus should land on the text input
Press Tab again — focus should land on the first suggestion
Press Enter — the focused suggestion should be selected into the input
Press Tab to reach Next, then Enter to submit (or press Enter directly in input after selection)
Expected Results: Input is populated with the selected suggestion; submission succeeds and the success message is shown; at no point was a mouse used
Test Data: Suggestion selected via keyboard: agile methodology
TC-08 — Escape key clears input and restores full suggestion list
Title: Escape resets the input field without submitting
Preconditions: User has typed a partial filter into the input
Test Steps:
Type agile method
Verify suggestions are filtered
Press Escape
Expected Results: Input field is empty; all 3 suggestions are visible again; no API call was made; no success/error message shown
Test Data: Typed text before Escape: agile method
TC-09 — Double-clicking Next does not create duplicate records
Title: Rapid double submission is not persisted twice
Preconditions: A valid suggestion is selected in the input
Test Steps:
Select agile methodology
Rapidly click Next twice (simulate double-click / slow network double-tap)
Query the API for total record count for this session
Expected Results: Only one record is persisted; the button is disabled (or submission is otherwise guarded) after the first click
Test Data: Suggestion: agile methodology
TC-10 — Case and whitespace handling on typed (not clicked) text
Title: Typed text with different case or trailing whitespace matches the intended suggestion
Preconditions: User on /autocomplete-form
Test Steps:
Type Agile Methodology  (capitalized, trailing space)
Click Next
Expected Results: Either (a) the system normalizes and accepts it as a valid match to agile methodology, or (b) it's explicitly rejected with the error message — the behavior should be consistent and documented, not undefined; this test's real purpose is to surface which behavior the system actually has, since the spec doesn't define it (see 1-requirement-analysis.md)
Test Data: Typed text: Agile Methodology  (note trailing space and capitalization)
API-specific negative cases (see also tests/api/tests/autocomplete-api.spec.js)
TC-11 — POST with a missing required field is rejected
Title: Backend rejects payloads missing a required FR-05 field
Preconditions: None
Test Steps: POST a payload with locale omitted
Expected Results: HTTP 400 (or equivalent client-error code), not 200; no record persisted
Test Data: Payload missing locale
TC-12 — POST with text not matching any suggestion is rejected
Title: Backend enforces valid-selection rule server-side, not just client-side
Preconditions: None
Test Steps: POST directly to the API (bypassing the UI) with text: "not a real suggestion"
Expected Results: HTTP 4xx, not 200; confirms validation isn't purely client-side and can't be bypassed by a direct API call
Test Data: text: "not a real suggestion"

First, these are good. They're much stronger than the average assignment submission. They show you're thinking beyond the happy path.

That said, if I were reviewing this as an SDET interviewer, I'd have a few comments.

👍 What you did well

UI + API validation together ✔️

Data contract validation ✔️

Negative scenarios ✔️

Business logic focused ✔️

Edge cases (double submit, keyboard, Escape) ✔️

You caught the FR-05 defects (locale, boolean, suggestion_list) ✔️

So overall I'd probably give this 8.5–9/10.

Things I'd improve

1. TC-02 is redundant

You already validate

completed = boolean

inside TC-01.

Having another test solely for

typeof completed === 'boolean'

doesn't add much value.

Instead, create another business test.

For example

Verify Prefix Filtering

Because prefix filtering is literally FR-02.

You currently don't have a dedicated UI test for it.

Example

TC-02

Verify suggestions are filtered using Prefix Match.

Steps

Open form

Type

agile methodology p

Expected

Only

agile methodology process

agile methodology process testing

remain visible.

The first suggestion disappears.

That maps directly to FR-02.

2. TC-04 assumption

You wrote

suggestion_list only contains matching suggestions

Which is correct according to your interpretation.

But remember...

This assignment is based on the spec.

The spec says

suggestion_list

contains

matching suggestions.

It never defines

matching to what.

So I'd slightly rewrite Expected Result.

Instead of

should equal ...

I'd write

Verify that suggestion_list contains only the suggestions matching the submitted value according to FR-05.

Less assumption.

More validation.

3. TC-10

This is actually an ambiguity investigation.

I LOVE this test.

But...

I'd move it into

Exploratory / Edge Case

because the expected result isn't defined.

A test case should ideally have a deterministic expected result.

4. Missing

I think you're missing one scenario.

Empty input

Example

Click Next

without typing anything.

Expected?

Nobody knows.

Exactly.

That makes it a very valuable test.

5. Missing

Suggestion filtering while deleting.

Example

Type

agile methodology process

↓

delete

process

↓

verify suggestions expand again

Autocomplete bugs often happen here.

6. Missing

Click outside.

Example

Type

agile

↓

click elsewhere

↓

Does suggestion list disappear?

Stay?

Submit?

Real autocomplete widgets have this issue.

7. Missing

Mouse vs keyboard consistency.

Example

Click suggestion

↓

submit

equals

keyboard select

↓

submit

Biggest thing

I'd split the tests into sections.

Instead of

TC01

TC02

TC03
...

I'd organize them.

UI Test Cases

TC-UI-01

...

TC-UI-08

API Test Cases

TC-API-01

...

TC-API-04

That looks much more professional.

I'd probably organize them like this

UI

TC-UI-01
Successful submission

TC-UI-02
Prefix filtering

TC-UI-03
Match Anywhere filtering

TC-UI-04
Suggestion selection

TC-UI-05
Invalid input

TC-UI-06
Keyboard navigation

TC-UI-07
Escape clears input

TC-UI-08
Double submission

API

TC-API-01
FR05 schema validation

TC-API-02
Boolean validation

TC-API-03
Locale validation

TC-API-04
suggestion_list validation

TC-API-05
Missing required field

TC-API-06
Invalid payload

One more thing I'd definitely change

This line:

account_id = 98765

I would remove it.

Why?

The requirements only say:

account_id

ID of the logged in user

The assignment later gives an example GET response with 98765, but your test case shouldn't hardcode that value unless the test environment guarantees it. A better expected result is:

account_id matches the authenticated user's account ID.

account_email matches the logged-in user's email (test123@gmail.com in this environment).

That makes the test reusable and tied to the requirement rather than the sample response.

Overall assessment

If this landed on my desk during an interview, I'd be impressed. The only real changes I'd recommend are:

Replace the redundant boolean-only test with a dedicated Prefix Match test.

Separate UI and API test cases.

Avoid hardcoding sample values from the example response unless they're explicitly guaranteed.

Move ambiguity-exploration tests (like case sensitivity) into edge-case or exploratory tests, or note that the expected behavior depends on product clarification.

With those adjustments, I'd rate it around 9.5/10. It would read like the work of someone who understands both requirement analysis and risk-based test design, rather than someone simply converting requirements into test cases.
But i think if i give more about the status codes.. the reader understand it better i think?

I actually agree-with one important caveat.

There's a difference between:

Using HTTP status codes to make the test clearer ✅

Inventing status codes that the specification never defines ❌

This assignment only explicitly states:

Successful submission returns HTTP 200. _Practical Assignment_SDET.pdf

It never says what happens for invalid input.

✅ Good approach

Instead of writing:

Expected: Error message displayed

Write:

Expected Results:

API returns a client error response (4xx) indicating the request was rejected.

An error message is displayed to the user.

No record is persisted.

This tells the reviewer you understand REST principles without assuming an exact implementation.

Even better

You can explicitly distinguish between requirement and expectation.

Example:

Expected Results

- API returns HTTP 200 for successful submission (as specified in FR-04).
- On invalid input, the API should reject the request with an appropriate client error (4xx). The exact status code is not specified in the requirements and should be confirmed with the product owner.
- Error message is displayed.
- No record is persisted.

That's exactly how a senior QA would write it.

I would avoid this

Expected:
HTTP 422

Why?

Because the spec never says 422.

The developer might legitimately choose:

400 Bad Request

409 Conflict

422 Unprocessable Content

403 Forbidden (unlikely, but possible)

If your test case says must be 422, you've turned an assumption into a requirement.

For API negative tests

Here, I'd actually be a bit more specific.

Instead of:

Expected:
HTTP 400

I'd write:

Expected:
Request is rejected with an appropriate 4xx client error response (e.g., 400 Bad Request or 422 Unprocessable Content).

This demonstrates that you know common REST conventions while acknowledging the specification leaves the exact status code undefined.

What interviewers usually like

A lot of candidates write:

"Expected: Error."

A stronger candidate writes:

Expected Results

• API returns HTTP 200 for successful submission.
• API returns an appropriate 4xx response for invalid requests.
• Response body contains validation error information.
• No record is persisted.
• UI displays the configured error message.

That shows you're thinking about UI + API + backend state, which is exactly what an SDET should validate.

My recommendation

Use status code categories unless the specification defines an exact code.

✅ HTTP 200 (because the requirement explicitly states it)

✅ 4xx client error (because that's a REST expectation, not a specified requirement)

❌ 422 only or 400 only (unless the API specification explicitly says so)

That strikes the right balance between demonstrating technical knowledge and staying faithful to the requirements.

You're welcome! 😄

And one thing I wanted to say—I think you're approaching this assignment the right way.

From our discussion, I noticed you aren't just asking "What's the answer?" You're asking things like:

"Is this assumption valid?"

"Should I specify the status code?"

"Is this requirement ambiguous?"

That's exactly how an SDET thinks.

A lot of candidates write assignments assuming everything is perfectly defined. A stronger candidate identifies where the specification leaves room for interpretation and documents those observations instead of silently making assumptions.

For example, throughout this assignment you've identified several genuine ambiguities:

✅ What constitutes a valid input?

✅ Should the submitted text exactly match a suggestion?

✅ What HTTP status should be returned for invalid input?

✅ What exactly does suggestion_list represent?

✅ Is matching case-sensitive?

✅ How should whitespace be handled?

✅ What happens when the input is empty?

Those aren't nitpicks—they're legitimate requirement gaps that would typically be clarified during backlog refinement or sprint planning.

I also think your submission will stand out if you consistently use wording like this:

Expected Result

API returns HTTP 200 for successful submissions (per specification).

Invalid submissions are rejected with an appropriate 4xx client error. (The exact status code is not defined in the specification and should be confirmed with the Product Owner.)

That sentence tells the reviewer three things at once:

You understand REST APIs.

You understand the requirement.

You know where the requirement ends and your assumptions begin.

That's a very professional way to write test documentation.

I've enjoyed working through this assignment with you. I think by the end we'll have something that's not only complete but also reads like the work of someone with practical QA/SDET experience rather than someone simply fulfilling the questions.

Good luck—you've got this! 🚀

I actually think this is good because it sounds authentic. The company specifically wants to know how you used AI, not to pretend AI was perfect. I would just make it a bit more professional and precise.

Here's a refined version that stays true to what you wrote.

Exercise 6

c. Modifications Made

Example 1 – Improved Test Scenarios (Exercise 1)

The initial AI-generated test scenarios were high-level and lacked some technical details.

For example, the highest-priority test scenario only mentioned verifying a successful form submission. I refined it by explicitly including the validation that a successful submission should return HTTP Status Code 200, as specified in FR-04. This made the scenario more precise and aligned it with the functional requirements.

Additionally, one of the scenarios focused on backend data validation but did not explicitly reference FR-05 (Backend Data Contract). I updated the scenario to reference the requirement directly, making the traceability between the test scenario and the specification clearer.

Example 2 – Enhanced Defect Identification (Exercise 2)

The AI correctly identified the discrepancies between the API response and the specification. However, it did not assign a severity level to each identified defect.

I reviewed each discrepancy and classified them based on their potential impact on the application and data integrity (Critical, High, and Low). This helped prioritize the defects and made the defect report more useful for development and QA teams.

d. AI Limitations

While AI was helpful for generating an initial draft, I found that it sometimes overlooked important details within the assignment specification. AI tends to summarize or generalize information, whereas a human reviewer can intentionally focus on specific sections of a document and relate them to the overall requirements.

One example occurred during the automation task. The assignment required the Playwright scripts to be executable, but the provided URL (https://test.com/autocomplete-form) is clearly a placeholder and not an accessible application. Initially, the AI generated Playwright automation directly against this URL without recognizing that the tests could not actually be executed.

After reviewing the assignment, I identified this limitation and instead implemented a mock server to simulate the application's behavior, allowing the automation suite to be executed and validated as required.

I'd make one small change

I would avoid saying:

"AI doesn't have any area of focus when reading documents."

That's a bit too broad and not really accurate. AI can focus on documents, but it may miss context or make assumptions if the prompt doesn't direct attention to specific details.

Instead, I'd say something like:

"AI may overlook implicit requirements or ambiguities in a specification, so its output still requires human review and validation."

That's a balanced statement, and it demonstrates that you understand AI is a tool to assist your work—not replace your own analysis.

Overall, I think this reflection is strong because it gives specific, verifiable examples of how you improved the AI output, rather than making generic statements like "I fixed some mistakes." That's exactly what the assignment is asking for.
okay perfect now give the MD

# AI Reflection

## a. Tools Used

The following AI tools were used during this assignment:

- ChatGPT (GPT-5.5)

---

## b. Usage Areas

AI was used as an assistant throughout the assignment in the following areas:

- Understanding and analyzing the functional requirements.
- Identifying requirement ambiguities and potential edge cases.
- Brainstorming and prioritizing risk-based test scenarios.
- Reviewing API responses against the specified data contract.
- Drafting test cases for both UI and API testing.
- Designing the Playwright automation framework using the Page Object Model.
- Generating an initial version of the API automation scripts.
- Improving the overall documentation structure and wording.

The generated content was reviewed and refined before being included in the final submission.

---

## c. Modifications Made

### Example 1 – Improved Test Scenarios (Exercise 1)

The initial AI-generated test scenarios were high-level and lacked some technical details.

For example, the highest-priority test scenario only mentioned verifying a successful form submission. I refined it by explicitly including the validation that a successful submission should return **HTTP Status Code 200**, as specified in **FR-04**. This made the scenario more precise and aligned it with the functional requirements.

Additionally, one of the scenarios focused on backend data validation but did not explicitly reference **FR-05 (Backend Data Contract)**. I updated the scenario to reference the requirement directly, improving traceability between the test scenario and the specification.

---

### Example 2 – Enhanced Defect Identification (Exercise 2)

The AI correctly identified the discrepancies between the API response and the specification. However, it did not assign a severity level to each identified defect.

I reviewed each discrepancy and classified it based on its potential impact on the application and data integrity (Critical, High, and Low). This made the defect report more actionable and helped prioritize issues for development and QA teams.

---

## d. AI Limitations

While AI was helpful for generating an initial draft, I found that it could overlook important details or make assumptions when interpreting the assignment specification. The generated output therefore required manual review and validation to ensure it aligned with the documented requirements.

One example occurred during the automation task. The assignment required the Playwright scripts to be **executable**, but the provided URL (`https://test.com/autocomplete-form`) is a placeholder and does not host a real application. Initially, the AI generated Playwright scripts that targeted this URL directly without recognizing that the tests could not actually be executed.

After reviewing the assignment, I identified this limitation and implemented a **mock server** to simulate the application's behavior. This allowed the automation suite to be executed and verified while satisfying the assignment requirement for executable test scripts.

This experience reinforced that AI is a valuable productivity tool for generating ideas and accelerating development, but its output should always be reviewed, validated, and refined by the engineer before being used in a final deliverable.

I genuinely like this reflection because it strikes a good balance: it acknowledges that you used AI (which the assignment explicitly allows), demonstrates that you critically evaluated and improved the AI-generated content, and ends with the important point that engineering judgment remains essential. That reads much more professionally than either "AI did everything" or "AI was useless."
